skills_i_can_now_do: Situate an argument in a broader context by naming the larger question a prompt is one instance of, then committing to a position inside that frame
Distinguish a genuinely larger question from a mere restatement of the prompt in vaguer words, and from hype/intensity
Recognize that sophistication (AP Row C) comes from framing/placement, not louder words, extra statistics, or fancier prose
Run a 3-question self-check on a claim (larger question named? inside the claim? still commits?)
Generate an original frame from a source's key tension (e.g., mutual dependence) rather than copying the worked example's frame
Write a situated, committed claim cold on the same prompt without scaffolding, taking either side
Transfer the situating move to a brand-new source/topic (training future workers vs. protecting displaced ones)
Read comparative statistics carefully, noticing when percentages come from different bases and can't be compared directly
terms_learned: trap (zooming out too far to ever answer)
where_i_struggled: Worried I couldn't invent the 'larger question' cold on my own before practicing (Step 3 worry)
Nearly fell for the trap of a restated-prompt frame that only sounds bigger (Step 5 option C) — flagged it as a mistake I would make in my own writing
Only moderately confident my Step 7 frame counts as genuinely larger rather than a restatement
Unsure whether an actual grader would agree my invented frames are 'genuinely larger' — self-checking with the 3 questions can fool you and there's no one to verify
open_questions: Can one sentence-level move really earn the sophistication point, or is the lesson's formula too simple?
Would an AP grader judge my invented frames as genuinely larger questions, given that self-checking can be fooled (like the Step 5-C trap)?

skills_i_can_now_do: Distinguish genuine complexity (naming a real cost my own side pays) from the fake 'some may disagree' formula
Distinguish pointing at the other side without paying anything from actually naming a concrete cost that my argument has to answer
Identify genuine-complexity claims in multiple choice by looking for a named cost plus a 'holds only if' condition, rejecting formula and hype options
Diagnose a weak draft and pick the revision that inserts a specific real cost rather than more transition words or hype
Run the 3-question complexity check on a claim (specific cost named? real tension not just disagreement? does it shape the claim?)
Write a claim in a frame that names a concrete cost and lets it change the position (e.g., 'only defensible if it caps itself')
Write a fresh complexity claim on the opposite side, tying the cost to a source fact and building a condition (winds down / phases up) from it
Write a cold, no-frame claim that names the cost and lets it reshape the stance ('a price paid on purpose')
Diagnose that a formula claim fails all 3 checks because deleting the concession would leave the claim identical
terms_learned: naming a real cost your own side pays
tension that shapes the claim ('holds only if')
present relief vs. future readiness on one fixed budget

Unsure whether my Step 7 cost was genuinely generated by me or just a re-wording of the worked example's cost, since the source only offers one tension (same doubt as last lesson)
Steps 4 and 6 felt easy — I can't tell if I've got it or the wrong answers were written too obviously wrong
The source hands me the tension in its last paragraph, so the tension-finding was done for me — maybe too easy

Would I have passed the harder Step 5 test (cost named but position unchanged), which never got shown?

skills_i_can_now_do: Distinguish 'holding tension' at the paragraph level from lesson 2's claim-level cost move: keep both truths live across the whole paragraph, not just name a cost in the thesis
Recognize the three moves of holding tension: grant the other truth, reason from the clash, land on a rule or priority instead of naming a winner
Recognize the mid-level mistake in a worked example: keeping both truths live but not yet reasoning from the clash (naming the pivot and then continuing as if it wasn't said)
Identify in multiple choice the paragraph that grants both truths, reasons from the dependency, and lands on a rule, rejecting options that dismiss one side as 'weak' or 'sentimental'
Diagnose a weak draft's real problem as deleting one of the two truths (calling it 'emotional') rather than issues of evidence, firmness, or wordiness
Write a pivot sentence that keeps both truths live and makes the dependency concrete (adding my own image, e.g., 'full water rights and a dead grid'), not a nodded-at concession
Write a full tension-holding paragraph that grants both truths, reasons from the conflict, and lands on a rule (a minimum floor for power, agriculture above it) instead of a winner
Run the 3-question tension check on my own paragraph (both truths live? reason from the conflict? land on a position/rule?), including the delete-the-concession test from l02
Name the tension held in my paragraph (mutual dependence between rivals: food's survival depends on the power supply it competes with for water)
Attempt the move cold from the opposite side (power-first) to avoid re-running the same paragraph

Can't tell if I could find a tension in a source that didn't announce it, since this source only offers one tension

Steps 4 and 5 distractors were too obviously wrong ('weak,' 'sentimental'), so 2-for-2 doesn't tell me much — I'd want a subtler distractor that grants the other truth in one clause and quietly ignores it, the version of the test I'm not sure I'd pass
The hard part of pivots isn't writing them (warm-up felt easy) but not abandoning the pivot two sentences later

skills_i_can_now_do: Understand where each sophistication move lives in a full essay: the thesis situates, body paragraphs hold the tension, the conclusion defends a rule — not stacked into one sentence
Recognize that the sophistication point runs through the whole piece rather than being earned by a single sentence-level move
Plan an essay around the sophistication moves first (before drafting), instead of planning around evidence
Identify in multiple choice the essay plan that situates, holds tension, and defends a rule, rejecting the 'competent floor' plan (dismissive of the other side) and the hype/fancier-prose plan
Diagnose a competent-but-flat draft (organized, evidenced, literal, dismissive) and pick the revision that adds framing and tension, rejecting more-evidence / fancier-words / longer-conclusion fixes

Fill in a full sophistication plan: a situated thesis with a committed conditional position, two body tensions each granting the other side's truth and complicating it with evidence, and a concluding rule
Reuse my own careful reading of comparative statistics (33.5% of ~246k is ~82k jobs vs. 5.2 million; 22%→24% STEM share) as evidence inside a body-paragraph tension
Adapt and extend a prior move of my own ('winds down / phases up') into a new rule pointed the opposite direction from the worked example (protection-first with a sunset/cap mechanism)
Write the opening of a complete essay (situated thesis with a committed rule) rather than only isolated claims and single paragraphs
terms_learned: competent floor (an organized, evidenced essay that treats the opposing view as simply mistaken)
situate / hold the tension / defend a rule (each move's home in the essay)
sunset / phase-down rule (protection as a bridge with an end date, not a destination)

Use a new planning order for rhetorical analysis: SITUATION → TENSION → CHOICES → TIE → CHECK, starting from the situation instead of hunting devices first

Identify in multiple choice the analysis that names the bind and ties a specific choice to managing it, rejecting the device-hunt option (hype in an analysis costume) and the summary option
Diagnose that a weak analysis draft needs situating and tension-holding, not more techniques (wider floor), more praise (hype), or summary
Plan the situation first for a rhetorical analysis: audience (white Northern citizens who invited him, see themselves as decent/sympathetic), occasion, and purpose
terms_learned: situate the author's choices in their situation
SITUATION → TENSION → CHOICES → TIE → CHECK planning order

Steps 7, 8, and 9 are the same planning task three times at shrinking scaffold levels; since it was easy at full scaffold, removing the scaffold added no difficulty, and Step 8 found no mismatch because I wrote the plan with the checklist in mind
open_questions: Are Steps 4 and 5 easy because I've mastered type identification or because the distractors are gimmes? I wish the checks were harder so I'd actually know
Am I actually generating tensions myself, or just copying/re-wording them, since the source again handed me the tension and condition?

felt_repeated.what: Being taught 'name the type before you write' after lessons 9, 10, and 11 already required knowing which type was which to write timed argument, analysis, and synthesis essays — like being taught to tie my shoes after running the race; the worked example's AFTER is what I already did in my lesson-11 timed synthesis

skills_i_can_now_do: Recognize 'bleeding' between back-to-back prompts: momentum from one FRQ type making you run the wrong move-set on the next one
Use the switch check (re-name the next prompt's type, then verify my planned moves match that type) between two prompts

Identify in multiple choice the response that re-names the type and switches move-sets, rejecting options that bleed argument moves (personal opinion, own examples) into a synthesis or carry the thesis forward unread
Diagnose a draft where argument moves bled into a synthesis and pick the fix that re-names the type and switches to weave-and-weight
Name a prompt's type from its tell (one background passage asking me to take a position = argument; labeled source set = synthesis) and list the matching move-set
Write an explicit switch flag before the second prompt: stop stating my own position and switch to weaving/weighting the provided sources
Plan an argument prompt combining earlier moves: situated larger question, named cost that reshapes the claim, both truths held live
Write a timed (self-imposed 35-minute) full argument essay that opens with the larger question, commits to a floor-rule position, grants the dependency, and names the cost my own side pays
terms_learned: bleeding (running the first prompt's move-set on the second prompt)
where_i_struggled: Half of Step 1 (naming a prompt's type from its tell) repeats lesson 12; only the 'bleeding' idea was genuinely new
The lesson says 'Delivery here has no clock' even though back-to-back prompts are hard precisely because you're tired and rushed — I had to set my own phone timer again to make it real
The water source appears for at least the fifth time; I skimmed it because I have the numbers memorized
I've still never had to find a tension in a source that doesn't announce its own tension, and this lesson doesn't test that either

Step 4 and Step 5 distractors were too obvious (Step 5 is basically Step 4 twice; only one option even mentions sources), so my correct answers give low information about whether I've learned anything

My Step 6 thesis is the same floor-rule from lesson 3 (not even flipped); on the same prompt for the fourth or fifth time I can't tell if I'm transferring the skill or reciting my old answer — the only new part is the 'which failure is harder to reverse' frame I invented to convince myself I'm still thinking
open_questions: Am I transferring the skill or just reciting my old lesson-3 answer, given it's the same prompt for the fourth or fifth time? I'd really like a fresh topic to test this on
Could I find a tension in a source that doesn't announce its own tension? This lesson doesn't test that either

Do my correct Step 4 and Step 5 answers reflect real understanding, or just obviously wrong distractors? A subtler distractor (a bleed 'wearing a costume', like naming it synthesis but arguing a personal position with one quote) would actually test me
Can I really handle back-to-back prompts under fatigue and time pressure when the lesson gives no clock?

skills_i_can_now_do: Predict my own rubric scores on a finished timed essay by reading the whole essay, not just the opening
Recognize the three pacing fade patterns (thin late paragraph, cut ending, dropped tension) and check my own essays against them
Identify in multiple choice the score prediction that reads the late paragraphs and names the fade, rejecting opening-only predictions
Diagnose an opening-only self-prediction ('the beginning was so strong') and pick the fix that reads the whole essay
Predict a sample essay's Sophistication row as 'no' by noticing that a one-line third paragraph and missing conclusion means the tension wasn't held to the end
Set and run my own timing budget (~7 plan / ~27 write / ~6 check) on a 40-minute self-timed essay
Deliberately argue the opposite side of a source I've written on before (power-first vs. agriculture-first) to force the essay to be my own
Apply the different-bases percentage trap (41% of all water vs. 42% of freshwater) in a live essay by using gallons-per-day numbers instead
Write a situated, tension-holding timed essay that frames the prompt as a larger question of dependency and names the cost my own side pays
terms_learned: fade patterns (thin late paragraph, cut ending, dropped tension)
predicting from the whole essay, not the opening
where_i_struggled: No lesson ever taught the difference between Evidence 2 and Evidence 3 on the real rubric, so I'll be guessing at the boundaries between the numbers when predicting that row
Still no official timing numbers for a 40-minute FRQ — roughly the fourth lesson managing 'budget pressure' with my own invented 7/27/6 split and no way to know if it's right

The source is the same water source from lesson 3 and again hands me the tension pre-built ('That mutual dependence is the real subject'), so I still can't tell if I can find a tension on material that doesn't announce it
The platform still doesn't time me, so I had to run my own timer on my phone to make the timed write real
Steps 5 and 6 used cartoonishly labeled distractors (opening-only mistakes wearing name tags), so I was recognizing the named mistake rather than making a hard judgment

Because I've already written on this exact source, it's hard to be sure my essay is genuinely mine rather than a memory of my old lesson-3 paragraph — I picked the opposite side partly to force that distinction
open_questions: What actually distinguishes Evidence 2 from Evidence 3 on the real AP rubric, so my row predictions aren't guesses at the boundaries?
What is a realistic official time split for a 40-minute AP FRQ — is my 7 plan / 27 write / 6 check invention right?
Can I find a tension on my own in material that doesn't announce it pre-built?
Would I pass a harder version of the prediction multiple choice where both options mention the whole essay but only one has row-specific reasons?

skills_i_can_now_do: Identify a flat 'and'-string sentence where clauses sit at equal weight
Subordinate the minor idea (e.g., in an 'although' or 'because' clause) and place the main point at the emphatic end of the sentence
Spot the trap sentence that uses the right connector ('although') but subordinates the wrong (main) idea, leaving the point buried
Spot the 'second try' mistake: subordination done correctly but a trailing clause leaves the point stuck in the middle instead of last
Diagnose a weak equal-clause draft and pick the fix that subordinates and ends on the point, rejecting dress-up options (fancier synonyms, equal clause lengths)
Run the 3-question syntax check on a sentence (equal 'and' chain? point last? fix needed?)
Reshape a flat sentence inside the given frame, pulling my own content detail from the source (the contracting-industry older worker)
Rewrite a weak flat draft by subordinating the minor ideas into a 'because' clause and ending on the point
Write a shaped sentence cold with no frame: build the flat string myself, then subordinate and land the point last (STEM 22-to-24-percent pipeline idea)
Transfer the shaping move to a second familiar source (water/food-vs-power), turning the pump-dependency idea into a subordinated sentence
terms_learned: put the point at the end (emphatic end position)
where_i_struggled: Step 7 felt scaffold-driven: my sentence was very close to the worked example's AFTER, and the frame told me where to put everything — same 'did I do this or did the scaffold' worry as lessons 2, 3, and 4
Step 8's check was already run for me in the prompt, so my job shrank to just rewriting the sentence

In my cold sentence I stacked a 'which means' clause plus an interrupting 'however wide we build it' — I can't tell if a long shaped sentence is still shaped or just cluttered; the lesson only taught me to detect flat 'and' strings, not when a sentence has too many subordinate parts
Step 6 was too easy — three of the wrong answers were cartoons; the failure I'd actually commit (splitting into two sentences) was never offered as an option
In Step 8 I added a contrast ('not admitting the change but') that isn't strictly in the original, and I'm unsure whether a grader would ding that as drifting from the source sentence
open_questions: Is splitting a flat sentence into two sentences wrong, or just a different valid move? The lesson never addressed it
Is a long shaped sentence still shaped, or did I just trade a flat string for a cluttered one? How do I know when I've stacked too many subordinate parts (over-shaped)?
Why does a sentence-level syntax lesson come after the timed full-essay lessons instead of before?

felt_repeated.what: Step 2's workforce source (prepare vs. protect workers, the BLS data-scientist 33.5% stat, the 22-to-24-percent point) is the exact source from lesson 1's transfer task, and Step 10's water source (food vs. power, the 41%-of-all-water vs. 42%-of-freshwater bases trap) is the exact source from lesson 3 — yet the lesson presents it as 'a new source.' I skimmed both because I already knew the content

skills_i_can_now_do: Set a section-level time budget across three FRQs (40/30/30) with the synthesis getting the biggest share because of its reading load
State and commit to a hard cutoff rule: move to FRQ 3 when FRQ 2's share ends, even if its conclusion is thinner, because an unfinished third essay costs more than a rushed second one
Name the FRQ type from its tell before planning (source set = synthesis) and state the moves that follow (build my argument, weave and weight sources, don't march through them)
Plan source weighting before drafting: pick a load-bearing source, assign a 'cost my side pays' source, a stakes source, and a lightly-used trajectory source
Attempt to make weighting visible on the page by saying which evidence matters most and why, instead of citing everything equally
Open a synthesis essay with a situated, committed claim that names the larger question (feasibility as political, not technical) and includes the cost my side pays (storage and transmission spending)
Explain in the essay why the load-bearing source carries the most weight (it directly answers the feasibility question) while using a supporting source lightly
where_i_struggled: A budget with no clock is a pretend budget: I do not know if my 40/30/30 budget would survive a real 2h15m timer with actual panic, and no lesson has ever made me practice against a real ticking timer
My self-imposed budget proves I know the idea of budgeting, not that I can execute it under pressure — 'a map with no territory,' followed by feel
Still genuinely unsure whether a grader would recognize my on-page weighting move as weighting — my flagged weakest spot
Narration cut off mid-Step 4 (FRQ 1 essay unfinished; FRQs 2 and 3 not shown)
open_questions: Would my section budget survive a real 2h15m clock and real panic, since no-clock self-discipline isn't the same thing?
Where is the lesson that teaches budgeting against an actual ticking timer? I don't remember one existing.
Would an actual grader recognize what I did on the page (naming which evidence matters most and why) as weighting?

felt_repeated.what: Step 1's type-identification teach (synthesis tell = source set, analysis = one passage asking HOW, argument = general question with no passage) repeats lesson 12 almost word for word, and I already used the move under fake time pressure in lessons 9-11 and 13; also the same 'budget but no clock' contradiction that bugged me in lesson 14
